text:
Greetings, authors and book lovers. I am sorry it has taken me so long to write--the last few months have been unusually busy. Luckily, the situation is starting to stabilize, and I am looking forward to going back to blogging and potentially more formal writing next year.

Since 2020 itself is already extraordinary, I have recently begun to consider how future writers will treat stories set in part or entirely this year. This reflection was not my brainchild; it was a question that was asked by my husband, and it immediately got my mind going.

I was reading The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue by V.E. Schwab, a highly recommended novel, earlier this week. It is the story of a woman, Addie, who sells her soul to live forever, and it follows her life in the 18 th century France and in the present-day New York City in 2014. As I was explaining the premise to my husband, he questioned when the book was set. That question was expanded to a larger one: how will writers treat the year 2020 in fiction?

How will 2020 eventually impact literature? Will it be a landmark, a moment that is often mentioned and discussed, or will it be an era that authors avoid mentioning? The pandemic has already been used as a central theme in some novels, especially in genres such as romance, but I have not read them yet. One can wonder what speculative genres would address this year: an urban fantasy in which supernatural struggles take place in abandoned cities, or alternate histories in which COVID-19 is caused by powers much greater than humans.

In addition to fantasy, literary fiction can also struggle with the emotional and social burden of the pandemic. Will COVID-19 be used as a plot device, or will authors give it a rich symbolic meaning? It is necessary to make it clear that this discussion does not diminish the seriousness of the pandemic and the terrible loss of life that it has brought to the world.

Narratively, 2020 is logistically challenging. Most of the world is locked indoors, so there is limited movement and exploration of characters. When fictional characters decide to disregard these restrictions, what will the readers think of them? The judgments we make in the real world at this age are under intense scrutiny and the same will probably be applied to fictional characters. These ethical and social conflicts, though, can be fertile soil of meaningful conversation and powerful narration.

metadata.notes: English contemporary fiction story about writing during 2020 - mixed AI/human version

composition.strategy: Balanced rewrite combining AI and human perspectives
